audienceSections.normal: In Anna and Ivan meeting at home, you will use [[grammar:Я + N]]. Put [[grammar:Я]] before your name or role to say who you are. Я студентка means ‘I am a female student.’ Russian normally has no present-tense word for ‘am’ here, so do not add one.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter presents [[grammar:Я + N]] in Anna and Ivan meeting at home. This is a present-tense zero-copula nominal clause. Predicate role nouns agree with the referent’s natural gender; contrastive а in А я преподаватель links the second identity.

audienceSections.normal: Put [[grammar:Я]] before your name or role to say who you are. Я студентка means ‘I am a female student.’ Russian normally has no present-tense word for ‘am’ here, so do not add one.
audienceSections.expert: This is a present-tense zero-copula nominal clause. Predicate role nouns agree with the referent’s natural gender; contrastive а in А я преподаватель links the second identity.

audienceSections.normal: Keep Cyrillic as the main text. In the reading help, an acute accent marks the stressed vowel; the accent is a study mark and is not normally written in everyday Russian.
audienceSections.expert: The support uses Cyrillic with a single acute accent on the stressed vowel of polysyllabic content words. It introduces no romanization and leaves monosyllables unmarked unless contrast requires it.

breakdown.normal:
Read each exact russian line, then check its natural English meaning. Watch for [[grammar:Я + N]] where it appears.

- Здравствуйте! — Hello!
- Привет! — Hi!
- Я Анна. — I am Anna.
- Я Иван. — I am Ivan.
- Я студентка. — I am a female student.
- А я преподаватель. — And I am a male teacher.
breakdown.expert:
These exact reading lines provide the distributional evidence for [[grammar:Я + N]]. The grammar section above analyzes the relevant morphology and syntax.

- Здравствуйте! — Hello!
- Привет! — Hi!
- Я Анна. — I am Anna.
- Я Иван. — I am Ivan.
- Я студентка. — I am a female student.
- А я преподаватель. — And I am a male teacher.
